Castanea sativa, commonly known as the sweet chestnut, is a deciduous tree species native to the mountainous regions of southern Europe. The sweet chestnut is valued for its edible nuts, which have been associated with various health benefits, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and immunomodulatory effects. The bioactive constituents of Castanea sativa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and triterpenes, which have been shown to possess antimicrobial and antiviral properties. The most common herbal preparations made from the sweet chestnut include tea, capsules, and tinctures, which are often used to support digestive health and boost the immune system.

The Fagaceae family is a part of the larger clade of flowering plants called the Angiospermophyta. Within the Fagaceae family, the Castanea genus is also closely related to the Quercus genus, which includes the oak trees.
